cards.title: The Map is Not
cards.body: The map is not the territory, a concept by Alfred Korzybski, highlights that our representations of reality are not reality itself.

cards.title: Occam's Razor
cards.body: Occam's Razor, a philosophical principle, states that the simplest explanation for a phenomenon is usually the most accurate one.

cards.title: The Tragedy of the Commons
cards.body: The Tragedy of the Commons, a concept by Garrett Hardin, explains how shared resources can be depleted due to individual self-interest.

cards.title: Pascal's Wager
cards.body: Pascal's Wager, an argument by Blaise Pascal, suggests that it is rational to believe in God because the potential reward is infinite.

cards.title: The Utility Monster
cards.body: The Utility Monster, a thought experiment by Robert Nozick, argues against the idea that moral decisions should be based solely on overall happiness.

cards.title: Moral Imperative
cards.body: The Moral Imperative, a concept by Immanuel Kant, states that moral laws should be based on reason and universal principles.

cards.title: Sorites Paradox
cards.body: The Sorites Paradox, also known as the paradox of the heap, raises questions about the nature of vagueness and categorization.

cards.title: The Mere Addition Paradox
cards.body: The Mere Addition Paradox, a thought experiment by Derek Parfit, challenges the idea that overall happiness is the only consideration in moral decisions.

cards.title: The Asymmetry
cards.body: The Asymmetry, a concept by Derek Parfit, suggests that our attitudes towards creating happy lives and preventing suffering are not symmetrical.

cards.title: The Repugnant Conclusion
cards.body: The Repugnant Conclusion, a thought experiment by Derek Parfit, suggests that a world with a large population of people living barely tolerable lives could be better than a world with a small population of very happy people.

options: The Map is Not the Territory

guesses.prompt: What is the minimum number of people that would make a world with barely tolerable lives better than a world with 100 very happy people, according to the Repugnant Conclusion?
guesses.explanation: The Repugnant Conclusion suggests that a large population of people living barely tolerable lives could be better than a small population of very happy people.
